title: The Controls
subtitle: How risk actually drops

hook.metaphor: Seatbelts, airbags, locks, ABS — controls are the engineered things that turn a hazard into a near-miss instead of a tragedy. In DRC, controls are how an identified risk actually goes down, not just gets documented.
hook.scenario: Two companies hold the same customer database. One encrypts it, scopes access to four people, and auto-deletes after 90 days. The other has it open on a shared drive 'for convenience.' Same data, wildly different risk — that gap is entirely controls.

learn: The core controls a builder should know: **access control** (who can touch what — least privilege), **encryption** (at rest and in transit), **data minimization** (collect/keep only what you need), **masking & tokenization** (show or store a safe stand-in instead of the real value), and **retention & deletion** (data has an expiry).

Crucial nuance for product-builders: a control can live at three levels. You can **implement** it (your product does it), **enforce** it (your product makes others do it / blocks violations), or **evidence** it (your product proves it happened). The same control means very different engineering at each level.
Controls map back to hazards from Stop 2: encryption blunts breach, minimization and retention blunt over-retention, masking blunts misuse. Picking controls is really *risk-to-control matching*.
buildBridge: So your product must be deliberate about whether it **implements, enforces, or evidences** each control — because 'we support encryption' means nothing until you say which of the three you actually do.

interactions.prompt: Defense in depth: click each control layer to see the hazard it blunts and how a product delivers it.

stages.label: Access
stages.risk: Breach & misuse from over-broad access — the cause of most real incidents.
stages.control: Role/attribute-based access control, least privilege, just-in-time grants.

stages.label: Encrypt
stages.risk: Readable data if storage or traffic is intercepted.
stages.control: Encryption at rest and in transit, with managed keys.

stages.label: Minimize
stages.risk: Over-collection — every extra field is extra blast radius.
stages.control: Collect and keep only what's needed; drop fields you can't justify.

stages.label: Mask
stages.risk: Real sensitive values exposed to people/systems that only need a stand-in.
stages.control: Masking and tokenization — show or store a safe surrogate.

stages.label: Retain
stages.risk: Over-retention — data you forgot to delete becomes tomorrow's liability.
stages.control: Retention policies with verifiable deletion across copies.

interactions.prompt: For a DRC product, sort each capability by the level it operates at.

items.label: Encrypt the data we store ourselves
Block any pipeline that writes unencrypted PII
Produce a report proving encryption was on all year
Auto-revoke access after 90 days of non-use

interactions.prompt: You can ship one control first for a product handling PII. Which gives the most risk reduction per unit effort?

options.label: Least-privilege access control
options.feedback: Strong choice. Most real incidents trace to over-broad access. Scoping who can touch data cuts breach and misuse at once.

options.label: A fancy anomaly-detection dashboard
options.feedback: Detection is valuable later — but it watches problems you could have prevented by limiting access in the first place.

options.label: Tokenize every field everywhere
options.feedback: Powerful but heavy, and it breaks lots of legitimate use. Great as a targeted control, costly as a blanket first move.

teachBack: In one line: *Controls are how risk actually drops — and for each one a product must decide whether it implements, enforces, or evidences it.*

checkpoint.question: Why does 'we support encryption' need a follow-up question?
options: Because a control can be implemented, enforced, or evidenced — and those are very different products
Because encryption only matters in transit
checkpoint.explanation: The level (implement / enforce / evidence) defines the actual engineering and the value to the buyer.

checkpoint.question: Choosing which control to apply is really…
options: Matching controls to the specific hazards you identified
Applying every control you can think of
Whatever the loudest customer asks for
checkpoint.explanation: Encryption→breach, retention→over-retention, masking→misuse. It's risk-to-control matching.
